speaker: interviewer
text: Irene Gregory on December 5th, 1996. This is cassette number four. You were telling me about your parents trying to find a ship out of Casablanca.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were thousands of others trying the same thing, so the ship they finally got was a little coastal steamer equipped for 75 people, registry Portuguese. They emptied it out. In the hold-- there was nothing in the hold except bunks, and therefore the ship was most unstable. We secured passage on this ship, and what usually takes a week or so took three weeks on that ship. The time of year was also horrific, because it was December, January-- January '42. There were storms in the Atlantic. And the worst thing was people were so scared. As soon as a ship showed up on the horizon, everybody would run to one side of the ship, and they were always sure it was a German submarine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it was also very ironic at times. There was a Swiss ship that came by. Can you imagine? I didn't know the Swiss had ships.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, most important of all, everybody was terribly sick. The ship was infested with rats. We had-- there were three young girls, myself and two others. And we took on the job of feeding six stowaways, young men who had no place to go. They had no visas, and stories have been written about these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had-- who knows what happened to them? They couldn't be sent back, and they didn't have any place to go. So they traveled around the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was my sacred duty to feed them. I was determined. I never got seasick, so no problem. And I made good friends with the officers. I got all kinds of goodies for them. My father was very distressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you intending to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The destination by then was New York, not Los Angeles. We were helped by [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We had no money. We arrived in New York with $10. The three of us had $10 between us, and worst of all, the sponsor we had was my mother's aunt. She died while we were on the seas. She was a very caring, wonderful, lady but died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York-- then. She had residences in Paris and in New York. She had-- her business was couture, haute couture, which means fancy clothing. And she had model houses, you know, where she had shows. And she was a very interesting woman. But-- so that was pretty scary. That news came across while we were on the seas in a telegram.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when we finally landed in Staten Island, they wouldn't let my father's family leave the customs area. Why? Because his name was Bernard Baruch. And Bernard Baruch's son was doing-- was in the Navy doing customs immigration duty. He saw the name, and he got really a little unsettled by that. So, after he cleared everything with his famous father, we were allowed off, but that almost killed my dad. He looked ashen by the time that was over. This was the son of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernard Baruch.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: The financier. Coincidence, but while we were still in Berlin, we were made to suffer from that. Hitler had an official paper called [GERMAN]-- no. What was it called? I guess it was called the [GERMAN]. And they kept making reference to this Jew with the same name, and of course international Jewry sticks together; therefore, this must be a bad person, or whatever-- cartoons, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] et cetera. But arrival in New York was less than pleasant for that reason. My mother's cousin put us up for a couple of nights, and I got a job the next day. And I got it through the Joint Committee, I think it was called-- Jewish Committee. And worked-- went to work for a family on Park Avenue, supposedly to take care of their little boy, but I ended up doing everything. I stayed with them for six months. They had a summer house in Connecticut, on the Sound. And by the time my English improved, I got out of there fast. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But my parents couldn't find work for a long time. I think a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel coming to a new country?

speaker: interviewee
text: People would say to me, aren't you happy to be here? Don't you feel relieved, dah dah dah dah dah dah? And I always said, of course, certainly. Oh yes, thank you very much. No, I didn't. I was furious. I wanted to get into the army. They wouldn't take me. I was a woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hated doing what I was doing. I was angry-- very angry-- and couldn't find enough room in my heart to be grateful to the United States, not then. That came later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you angry about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact that they were so slow and-- and did not admit people. We knew, because it happened to us. We were just super-lucky. I think luck has a lot to do with the fact that we got out. By then, it wasn't money anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were on your way to New York, did you make any stops along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Cuba. People without an American visa got off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get off the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but what happened was so callous and so awful. I remember watching in horror while people were screaming. These people maybe had a suitcase or two, and they picked up these suitcases with cranes, and the suitcases, the string or locks or whatever, would open up and everything would fall out into the water. And nobody made an effort to be careful. That is an image that I carried for a long time. Here they are. These poor people have saved very little, and then that happens. It was not nice. And these images stay with you. You know, it's difficult to shake. I-- don't think that I spent my life thinking about these things. I didn't. I had to get on with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned-- I believe you mentioned about Pearl Harbor, leaving after Pearl Harbor. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: We did. We left after Pearl Harbor. No one thought we would make it because of the U-boats in the Atlantic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear about Pearl Harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: That came over the radio. We listened to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] who broadcast from England. We were very well informed, behind drawn curtains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember hearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that America was betrayed, and what happened in Pearl Harbor, and surely they would now not hesitate any longer to enter the war in Europe. And that's indeed what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to work. We had a room in a boarding house on the Upper West Side. My father desperately tried to connect somehow, and it didn't work for him for a long, long time. Although he was fluent in English, so that wasn't a problem. He might have-- don't forget, the country was gearing up for war, and so anything that he knew how to do was not useful. And my mother had learned a trade. She had learned how to make neckties. Nobody was interested in neckties at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I made $40 a month for a long time, for about six months, and I gave it to them. I never took a day off because I didn't know anybody. And well, you know, the typical things happened to me. Like, you go down to a store. And when you're young-- I was 19-- you walk into a store, and I wasn't that bad looking, I suppose, and they always teased me like crazy. It was like running the gauntlet, or whatever you call that. But I did not have time to think about anything except to make that money and give it to my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, time went by without a social life, without-- it was an extension of what happened before. I did meet my first friends in America. No, my first friend in America was a black college girl from Atlanta. She worked for the same family, in the summer house, and she told me-- we talked through the night and she told me so much about this country. She was a staunch American, but black, and therefore she had a direction, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a certain bias. And-- but I felt the same way. I felt great kinship with her. And of course, that was just the beginning. But for some reason, I must have known a lot about America. I remember practicing a TH and how to roll the R on shipboard, because that was not part of German. And my father wrote pages and pages of grammar for me. I learned English. I was fluent within a year. It wasn't hard. I went to work for other things later, and life changed, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? By having freedom, finally, to see plays, to go to a movie, to wear nice things to-- I remember my first dress, I got caught in a hurricane, and it shrank, and I cried bitter tears. The only dress I had-- I needed it for work. And again, I remember supporting my parents, mostly emotionally by then, because the money somehow was enough. $40 A month was probably basic. But we moved to Queens, had a little apartment. And my father eventually found work. And I went to work in the same industry, actually. I worked for the custom shop, for-- Sulka and Company hired me because of my languages, which I never used. But I loved working there. It was a fabulous store. First woman they hired-- the men all went to war, and I was told, you know, you live in a euphoria, a self-made euphoria.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me that if the man survives whose job I took, I would have to give up my job to him when he comes home. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, sure. But what young person thinks so far ahead? Meantime, I was learning the business, and it was great, really wonderful. And I was introduced to my fiance then. What kind of business was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was haberdashery, custom. They are in business still, and very nice business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your fiance?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was arranged. [LAUGHS] The president of the company asked me if I was interested in meeting a respectable gentleman. And I said, well, why not? And he said, will you be chaperoned? And it took all I had not to burst out laughing. He was a little behind the times then already. That was 1945-- no, yeah, '45. Come on. But, he was also a refugee from Germany and an attorney. And, well, anyway, after I lost my job, I left New York, and I left him. I left my parents. I came to the West Coast, struck out on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the fiance?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's almost 90. We are in touch, although he never, until just recently, recognized my husband, because he always ignored him-- very funny story. But we're good friends. We talk to each other. We have a friendship, which is fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you dating then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to San Francisco? Oh, yes. I was 24 by then. I wanted to go to school, and I realized that I need to go to work first-- make some money and then go to school. So, I applied for three jobs, and I got them all. And it was easy for me. I was already in the business world, so I knew. And I chose the wrong thing. I went to work for United Airlines on Union Square and loved it. I had a little apartment in San Francisco on Scott and Fulton and a cat. And I met some wonderful new people-- young people, Americans. And yet, way deep down under, I always knew I would probably not marry an American, because I had baggage, and I knew it. You know, I had experiences that Americans-- they seemed very young to me-- inexperienced and immature. After all that I'd been through, I felt that I needed someone that would understand me, and that happened. I met Boris the first weekend I was on the west coast. We just had a lark. We met on Malibu Beach at a beach party. And I was very sure of myself in those days, and he was too. He had been through the war, almost five years, wounded. And we were at a point-- He was going to Berkeley, UC Berkeley, and he was at a point in life where he probably glanced at marriage, but not seriously. I wasn't either. We just met.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married after six dates. That was it. We both knew that we wanted to build a life together. His family was in China. Mine was on the east coast. And we decided we're going to have kids, and we're going to work hard, and we're going to enjoy. And that's what happened-- very nice, good story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had he been in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came in '39, and when the war broke out for America, he was-- he signed up. He wasn't drafted. He signed up. He became a citizen in six weeks. See, they wouldn't do it for me, but they did it for him. Then I wanted to work for the United Nations. I was really furious for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Furious about what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The things that were happening in Europe, and what it took to make America finally wake up.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sooner than we planned. The first one was born after we were married two years. And then I was, like he said, barefoot and pregnant for about eight years. We had four children, and we made the decision that he would go to work, and I would take care of the kids. That was the tradition. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it worked well. Did your children ever ask you about your war experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a lot. I never mentioned much to them, because I wanted to shield them from the rather dark experiences, and I asked them many times if I weighed heavy on them, and they always said no. You can ask them yourself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think anything is to be gained from a negative outlook.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any other Americans ask you about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Oh, absolutely. My young friends who were all feeling the way I did, for one thing, and I had a lot of wonderful, wonderful friends that have dispersed over the years. They went to war. They moved. Coming to America was so incredibly different from Europe. People were footloose. They had no worries like I had. And the experience was like being reborn, practically.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you tell your friends about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they asked me, I told them. But by then, a lot of people had much worse stories. So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, you have to get on with life, and you don't like to-- I didn't want to dwell too much on all these things. They got in the way, because life was tough every day. I had a long commute. I was living an American life. I thought it was not very fascinating life. I had wished for more. My aim had been to become a pediatrician. That never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have the luxury of university. After the children were grown, I went to school. I returned to-- returned-- I never had been. I resumed formal education, and maybe I'm not strong enough for completing it all, but I could have gone further than I did. But I loved it. I still go to school. I think I'll never stop. I think the only thing no one can take away from you is your-- what you carry in your head. I stil-- that I told my children. And they all are college graduates. I made sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Happy times. We all had-- we were all GI-- GI families, and we did this together. We lived in something called the-- the Virgins Paradise in Oakland. There was a string of barracks for the WAVES-- 27th Street and Telegraph, about that area. It's all gone. I mean, there are streets there now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we all moved in there together and raised babies. It was incredible. It was really fun. We were young. We were strong, and we never-- I don't think I ever was tired. What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for two years for Dun Bradstreet as a credit reporter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, in those days, you couldn't work if you were seven months pregnant, so they gave you a handshake and said, it has been nice knowing you. All those things have changed, but by then Boris graduated, and he began to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get involved with the community here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not just then. I can honestly say that the majority of our friends were Jewish, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but not formally-- no time, no sense, no roots. Temple Sinai was just across the way. It never dawned on me to even think about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't need the synagogue for solace.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was fiercely independent. We both were. And we were just determined to stick it out and make it, and a teacher made $3000 in those days a year and got paid once a month. So how do you stretch that? You know, this kind of thing was more important than anything else. We didn't have really time for anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we decided, we'll just have these babies, and hopefully they will grow up, and we can indulge. When the children became old enough for Sunday school, we enrolled them at Sinai. And Boris taught ethics at Sinai. I got myself on the board. I've always-- never had enough money to just write a check. And I find that charity, Tzedakah, is really better. When you put yourself on the line, give of yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your ideas about religion change at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: At one point in time, I hated being Jewish, and that subsided. What's so great about being Jewish? My God let me down. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the idea that we are a chosen people is a bunch of propaganda, I thought. Chosen for what? You know, I was a very angry person for a long time. But I kept it well hidden. I had a lot of fun. Our kids grew up just fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we took up a hobby, family hobby. We went sailing all together. They all became sailors. And competition weekends with like-minded couples-- we shared nothing but the sport in common. My past was my private zone. And I kept it to myself. You can make choices like that. Maybe it was wrong. I don't-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, now? Well, it started with my number two son, who is incredibly Jewish-minded. He would rather live in Israel than anyplace else, and he is very active in UJA, and he is involved. He lived on a kibbutz, Kibbutz Eilot for a year and has lots of friends in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he heard about this, he says, Mom, why don't you consider telling what you have to tell? And I said, well, it's been a long time, and I don't remember enough. He said, still, they may be able to use it. And then if there are archives, if there's anyone looking for you, just think of that. So, at first it didn't appeal to me at all. He said, here, take this number. Call it. [LAUGHS] So I did. And what did she tell me? My goodness, we got 10,000 phone calls. I wasn't exactly unique.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at first it drove me crazy. I went to bed, and I took a nap. I don't think I was ready. And now it's been a few months. Time has gone by. I don't mind. It's OK. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There were years, much longer ago, when this would have been more than difficult for me because of the memories. But it's been over 50 years. It's been 55 years, so the horror kind of pales, and you tend to sweep it aside. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not that I haven't been active in anything I can get my hands on, pretty much, for Jewish causes. And I'm proud of that. I really went to work at Jewish Family Service, and I was there every day. I took it real seriously, for six years. But then, you know, you know when it's enough. I think when it becomes a burden, you should go. Let somebody else do it. And that's what happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you were angry a lot. Do you know what that was about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I know exactly what that was about.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What people were doing to each other in Europe, man's inhumanity towards his fellow man-- I was old enough to understand those things, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] knowing that America could have ended the war sooner and saved a lot of people, knowing that they were turned away from America. The [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] broadcasts were called-- what were they called? Free France-- Free France. We knew a lot. And all you can do-- you are unable to do anything about it, but you can be angry. Was there anything else you wanted to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel very fortunate that I escaped like that and built a wonderful family. We have eight grandchildren. We have no discord in our family. I met this incredible guy. And my parents said, we don't marry Russians. That was the welcome for Boris.

speaker: interviewee
text: So on his mustering out-pay and my last flight ticket from United, we went to New York and got married in a snow storm. And nobody said a word. Everybody said that that's not going to last, because we were very different people, and here we are. Next year, it's 50 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think our kids are terrific people. They're very involved. My number one son is an active member of the Board of Temple Sinai in Palm Desert and very charitable. They all are. I'm happy. It turned out all right. Then, of course there was another huge upheaval in my life. We brought 11 people out of Russia, and they were our responsibility-- my husband's family. Saturday, I'm giving them a Hanukkah party. And, you know, it's wonderful to see people indulging in their religious beliefs, without being persecuted. That's a joy. I don't have to hide anything. It's neat. I don't take it for granted, either. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I like-- you need to-- I think that, from a very young age on, if you don't show respect to young children or to your fellow man, you have nothing. Respect is the base of everything, and then maybe love, and then maybe obligations and whatever else follows. But if you don't respect one another, you don't have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I hope it helps in some way.
